race_name: Taiwan KOM Challenge — The Ascent to the Clouds

top_categories.workout_context: 10,745ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Taiwan KOM Challenge — The Ascent to the Clouds' demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before October.
At mile 60 of 93, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 93 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
116ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.

Taiwan KOM Challenge — The Ascent to the Clouds' paved mountain road — sea level to 3,275m through forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 93 miles.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Taiwan KOM Challenge — The Ascent to the Clouds. Train it until it feels boring.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 10,745ft of climbing on paved mountain road — sea level to 3,275m through does to your power file on race day.
Short, max efforts are unavoidable. Punchy climbs, gap closures, attacks on paved mountain road — sea level to 3,275m through. If you haven't trained them, you'll crack.
At Taiwan KOM Challenge — The Ascent to the Clouds, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
Two threshold sessions per week without the recovery cost of VO2max work. For 93 miles, sustainable FTP gains matter more than peak power.
How long can you hold 105% FTP before you blow? At Taiwan KOM Challenge — The Ascent to the Clouds, that number decides whether you bridge or get dropped.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for Taiwan KOM Challenge — The Ascent to the Clouds’s 93 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.
race_overlay.altitude: Taiwan KOM Challenge — The Ascent to the Clouds with 10,745ft of climbing, much of it above 8,000ft demands altitude preparation. Arrive 5–7 days early for acclimatization. Expect 5–15% power reduction at altitude. Increase iron intake 4 weeks out. Hydrate aggressively — altitude increases fluid loss by 20–40%. Temperature drop of 20-25C from coastal start to alpine summit — you start in summer and finish in winter; High-altitude cold at 3,275m — wind chill can drive effective temperatures well below freezing.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on VO2max, Race Simulation, and Durability to prepare you for 93 miles of paved mountain road — sea level to 3,275m through taroko gorge in Yilan to Wuling Pass, Taiwan.
